coredata.dc:title: Quantification of zearalenone in mildewing cereal crops using an innovative photoelectrochemical aptamer sensing strategy based on ZnO-NGQDs composites
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Lijun
Liu, Xiaohong
Ma, Shuai
Li, Libo
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Zearalenone (ZEN) is highly toxic to humans, and therefore, development of sensitive and effective methods for ZEN quantification in cereal crops is particularly important. Here, an innovative photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor based on simply in-situ conjugated composites of zinc oxide-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (ZnO-NGQDs) was constructed. On addition of NGQDs, the composites displayed higher PEC signal with 8.8-fold enhancement than pure ZnO nanoparticles. A sensitive and selective PEC aptasensor was fabricated by combining the composites with ZEN aptamer, which yielded an excellent analytical performance for ZEN detection, with a wide linear range of 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10−7 g mL−1 and a low detection limit of 3.3 × 10−14 g mL−1. Good recoveries were obtained using the PEC aptasensor, which were consistent with those obtained using the national standard method (HPLC-MS). Finally, ZEN in mildewing cereal crops was monitored with the PEC aptasensor, exhibiting good potential for application in cereal crops for early diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mildew process monitoring
Photoelectrochemical aptasensor
Zinc oxide-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Zearalenone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of ZnO-NGQDs composites
Construction and characterization of the PEC aptasensor
Analytical performance of the PEC aptasensor
Stability, selectivity, and reproducibility of the proposed PEC aptasensor
Investigating ZEN concentration in mildewing cereal crops

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation of the ZnO-NGQDs composites. (B) Schematic illustration of the PEC aptasensor based on ZnO-NGQDs composites for ZEN detection.

(A) TEM image of the ZnO-NGQDs composites; Top illustration: HRTEM image of ZnO in ZnO-NGQDs composites; Bottom illustration: HRTEM image of NGQDs in Zn-NGQDs composites. (B) Elemental distribution mapping of ZnO-NGQDs composites and (C) mapped elements, including Zn, O, C and N. (D) XRD and (E) XPS of pure ZnO nanoparticles and ZnO-NGQDs composites. (F) Zn 2p core-level photoelectron spectra of pure ZnO nanoparticles and ZnO-NGQDs composites.

(A) Photocurrent responses of (a) ZnO/ITO, (b) NGQDs/ITO, (c) ZnO/NGQDs/ITO and (d) ZnO-NGQDs/ITO. Condition: 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.5, potential: 0 V. (B) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of ZnO-NGQDs, Inset: the corresponding Tauc’s plot. (C) Schematic diagram of the potential photocurrent enhancement mechanism.

(A) PEC responses at different modification steps in 0.1 M buffer solution at pH 7.5. (B) EIS Nyquist plots of (a) ITO, (b) ZnO-NGQDs/ITO, (c) Apt/ZnO-NGQDs/ITO, and (d) ZEN/Apt/ZnO-NGQDs/ITO in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.

(A) PEC responses and (B) Linear calibration for ZEN determination between the decreased photocurrent value and logarithm of ZEN. Condition: 0.1 M buffer solution at pH 7.5, potential: 0 V. (C) Stability of the proposed PEC aptasensor in the absence and presence of ZEN under consecutive measurements. (D) Long-term stability measurements of the proposed PEC aptasensor for two weeks. (E) Comparison of the PEC aptasensor response to 1 ng mL−1 ZEN and 100 ng mL−1 FB1, AFB1, OTA, and their mixture. (F) Repeatability of ZEN detection in 6 parallel tests.
Determination of ZEN in cereal crops (corn, barley, and rice flours) with the proposed PEC aptasensor and the HPLC-MS method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Quantification of zearalenone in mildewing cereal crops using an innovative photoelectrochemical aptamer sensing strategy based on ZnO-NGQDs composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Research Center for Agricultural Standards and Testing, Risk Assessment Laboratory for Agro-Products (Beijing), Ministry of Agriculture, Beijing Municipal Key Laboratory of Agriculture Environment Monitoring, No. 9 Middle Road of Shu Guang Hua Yuan, Haidian Dist., Beijing 100097, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Research Center for Agricultural Standards and Testing, Risk Assessment Laboratory for Agro-Products (Beijing), Ministry of Agriculture, Beijing Municipal Key Laboratory of Agriculture Environment Monitoring, No. 9 Middle Road of Shu Guang Hua Yuan, Haidian Dist., Beijing 100097, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zearalenone (ZEN) is highly toxic to humans, and therefore, development of sensitive and effective methods for ZEN quantification in cereal crops is particularly important. Here, an innovative photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor based on simply in-situ conjugated composites of zinc oxide-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (ZnO-NGQDs) was constructed. On addition of NGQDs, the composites displayed higher PEC signal with 8.8-fold enhancement than pure ZnO nanoparticles. A sensitive and selective PEC aptasensor was fabricated by combining the composites with ZEN aptamer, which yielded an excellent analytical performance for ZEN detection, with a wide linear range of 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10−7 g mL−1 and a low detection limit of 3.3 × 10−14 g mL−1. Good recoveries were obtained using the PEC aptasensor, which were consistent with those obtained using the national standard method (HPLC-MS). Finally, ZEN in mildewing cereal crops was monitored with the PEC aptasensor, exhibiting good potential for application in cereal crops for early diagnosis.

ce:keywords.ce:section-title: Chemical compounds used in this study

ce:keyword.ce:text: Zinc oxide-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Zearalenone (ZEN), a secondary metabolite of Fusarium, has been reported in most cereal crops, including rice, corn, barley, wheat, etc. (Chauhan, Singh, Sachdev, Basu, & Malhotra, 2016; Li, Yang, Mao, Hong, & Du, 2019; Wu et al., 2018). ZEN pollution has a considerable effect on the quality of agricultural products and food safety, and long-term exposure to ZEN poses a significant threat to human health, as it is teratogenic, carcinogenic, and neurotoxic (Lin & Guo, 2016). Considering these side effects, ZEN has been recognized as a group III carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (Zhang et al., 2017), and ZEN concentration in cereal crops must be below a certain limit. For example, the maximum acceptable residue level of ZEN in wheat and corn is 60 μg kg−1 in China (Wu, Xu, Chughtai, Jin, & Irudayaraj, 2017). Therefore, developing sensitive and selective analytical methods for detecting ZEN in cereal crops is particularly important.

To date, numerous chromatography-based methods, including high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Soleimany, Jinap, & Abas, 2012), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Kinani, Bouchonnet, Bourcier, Porcher, & Ait-Aissa, 2008), and ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) (Ma, Wang, You, & Wang, 2019), have been employed for accurately detecting ZEN in cereal crops. These analytical methods have a high sensitivity and good accuracy; however, they require complex sample pretreatments, expensive instruments, and highly skilled technicians. In contrast, immunoassays, such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering immunoassay (Liu et al., 2014; Guo et al., 2019) and electrochemical immunoassay (Sadrabadi, Ensafi, Heydari-Bafrooei, & Fazilati, 2016), are preferred for ZEN detection, due to their easy-to-operate instrumentation and low technical requirements. However, they are highly dependent on the use of antibodies, which can be unstable and expensive. Recently, aptamer-based sensors have been developed as alternatives to antibodies for ZEN detection, because aptamers are easy to modify and can bind to ZEN specifically (He & Yan, 2019). Presently, ZEN aptasensors are based mainly on electrochemical, fluorescent, and electrochemiluminescence methods (He & Yan, 2019; Luo et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019), and aptamer-based photoelectrochemical (PEC) methods have not yet been reported for ZEN detection. Thus, developing a PEC aptasensor for sensitive and selective quantification of ZEN seems appropriate.

Recently, the PEC method has been widely applied for mycotoxin analysis, owing to its low cost, high detection speed, and good performance. For a typical PEC system, photosensitive materials with high photoelectric conversion efficiency are essential for reliable analysis (Zhao, Xu, & Chen, 2015). Presently, semiconductor nanomaterials, such as tungsten oxide (WO3), titanium dioxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), etc., are widely used in PEC detection (Liu et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015). The applicability of ZnO (band gap: 3.20–3.37 eV) in photocatalysis and photovoltaic cells has been studied extensively, owing to its stability against photocorrosion, and high photoelectric and photocatalytic activities (Liu et al., 2014). However, the wide band gap of ZnO can lead to extremely low visible-light absorbency, further reducing the photoelectric response and limiting its application in PEC sensing systems.

To overcome the above-mentioned issues, graphene has been widely employed as a charge transport medium in PEC sensing strategies, owing to its narrow band gap, good electrical conductivity, and excellent photochemical properties (Kim et al., 2015; Shim et al., 2016). Due to the electronic interaction between narrow-band-gap and wide-band-gap semiconductors, graphene acts as a good sensitizer, and can effectively promote photoinduced electron transfer and hinder charge recombination during the electron transfer process of the PEC reaction. However, poor water solubility of graphene results in limited efficiency when compared to semiconductor oxide photosensitizers. Recently, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been shown to be excellent electron mediator and supporter alternatives, owing to their high biocompatibility (Yeh, Teng, Chen, & Teng, 2014). For example, Zhu et al. employed NGQDs to prepare heterojunctions, which were more efficient in charge separation than pure material under visible-light irradiation (Zhu et al., 2017). To the best of our knowledge, the fabrication of ZnO-NGQDs composites for PEC aptamer sensing of ZEN has not yet been reported.

In the present work, novel ZnO-NGQDs composites were prepared using a one-step in-situ conjugate method. As the introduction of NGQDs can efficiently inhibit the recombination of charge-hole pairs and improve photoelectric conversion of ZnO, the PEC signal obtained using the ZnO-NGQDs composites was 8.8 times higher than that obtained with ZnO nanoparticles. Based on this, an innovative PEC aptamer sensing strategy was fabricated, which demonstrated high sensitivity, and good selectivity and reproducibility. In addition, the recoveries obtained using the PEC aptasensor were at par with those obtained with the national standard method (high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, HPLC-MS). Finally, the concentration of ZEN produced during the mildewing process of cereal crops (including corn, rice, and barley flours) was investigated, illustrating the tremendous potential of aptamer sensing in ZEN quantification, and its potential application in agricultural monitoring.

N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl) carbodiimide (EDC), ZEN, fumonisin B1 (FB1), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and ochratoxin A (OTA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and ammonium citrate tribasic were provided by Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China).

The ZEN aptamer, 5′-GGA ATT CTT GAT GTT GCC TGG GAT TGT TTG GGC CTT GTG TTT TCT TCC GTT CCA ACT TAG TAG GAT CCC GAA-NH2-3′, was obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The aptamer stock solution was diluted in Tris-HCl (pH 7.4).

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed using FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (American), and element distribution mappings were developed using an energy dispersive spectrum spectrometer. Images were also obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JSM-7800F, JEOL, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were obtained with the UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using a Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument (VG Scientific, UK), coupled with Al Kα radiation. PEC current measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed using CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China), which employed a standard three-electrode system with modified ITO as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum (Pt) wire as the counter electrode. In addition, an Xe lamp (PLS-SXM300/300UV, Perfect Light, Beijing) acted as the visible light source, and the current–time (I-t) method was used for the entire PEC experiment. All PEC measurements were tested in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution, while EIS measurements were studied in 0.1 M KCl with 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−.

NGQDs were first synthesized as previously described (Yin et al., 2016). Then, ZnO-NGQDs composites were prepared using a modified method, as shown in Fig. 1 A (Son et al., 2012). Briefly, zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O) and NGQDs were added to 30 mL N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and stirred to form a steady precursor, which was then heated at 95 °C. Once the mixed solution turned milky white, the suspension was washed thrice with ethanol and ultrapure water. Finally, the obtained ZnO-NGQDs composites were dried and stored in powder form. For comparison, ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized using the same method, but without the addition of NGQDs.

The fabrication process of the PEC aptasensor is shown in Fig. 1B. Prior to modification, the ITO electrode (6 mm) was successively washed with NaOH (1 M), ultrapure water, and ethanol for 10 min. The electrode was then modified using 4 mg mL−1 ZnO-NGQDs solution. Then, 0.005 M NHS and 0.01 M EDC were added to activate the carboxylic groups of the NGQDs (Xu et al., 2017). This was finally combined with the ZEN aptamer by a condensation reaction to fabricate the PEC aptasensor.

ZEN solutions with different concentrations were coated onto the surface of the PEC aptasensor and incubated for 50 min. Due to the specific interaction between ZEN and its aptamer, the PEC signal of the aptasensor would decrease due to the effect of steric hindrance. Therefore, PEC signals in the absence and presence of ZEN were recorded for ZEN quantification.

The developed PEC aptasensor was used to analyze ZEN in cereal crops (corn, rice, and barley flours) purchased from the Auchan supermarket (Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China). Extraction was performed according to previously described methods (Luo et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2018). The supernatant solution was then diluted to different concentrations (1, 10, 50, and 100 ng mL−1) with the buffer solution, and then stored at 4 °C until further analyses. In order to verify the accuracy and reliability of the PEC aptasensor, ZEN was also detected using the national standard method (HPLC-MS, GB 5009.209–2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of ZnO-NGQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM was used to characterize the morphology of ZnO-NGQDs composites. It is evident from Fig. 2 A (left panel) that NGQDs, with a relatively narrow size distribution and average diameter of 3.2 ± 0.2 nm, were uniformly coated on the surface of ZnO nanoparticles. HRTEM images revealed that the ZnO-NGQDs composites had a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm (Fig. 2A, top right panel) and 0.21 nm (Fig. 2A, bottom right panel) corresponding to the distance between two (0 0 2) planes of the hexagonal ZnO phase (Son et al., 2012) and the (1 0 0) facet of graphitic carbon (Qu et al., 2014), respectively. Fig. 2B shows the elemental distribution mapping spectra of the ZnO-NGQDs composites, with each element (Zn, O, C, N) being uniformly distributed in the composites (Fig. 2C). These results demonstrate that ZnO-NGQDs composites had been prepared successfully.

The XRD spectra of ZnO nanoparticles and ZnO-NGQDs composites are shown in Fig. 2D. It can be seen that eight diffraction peaks of pure ZnO nanoparticles, corresponding to (1 0 0), (0 0 2), (0 0 1), (1 0 2), (1 2 0), (1 0 3), (1 1 2), and (2 0 2) planes, were obtained (JCPDS No. 36-1451). After introducing the NGQDs, the diffraction peak positions of the ZnO-NGQDs composites corresponded to those of pure ZnO nanoparticles; however, their intensities were relatively lower. These results indicate that NGQDs do not lead to new crystal orientation or ZnO preferential orientation changes; however, the crystallinity of ZnO-NGQDs composites is reduced. In addition, no significant diffraction peaks of carbon species were observed in the ZnO-NGQDs composites, which may be attributed to the low concentration of NGQDs (Liu et al., 2014).

XPS technology was used to evaluate the functional groups on the surface of the ZnO-NGQDs composites. It can be seen in Fig. 2E that the survey XPS spectrum of the ZnO-NGQDs composites showed the presence of Zn, O, C, and N core-elements. The atomic ratio of C 1s to N 1s (C/N) in the ZnO-NGQDs composites was 24.56%, which is considerably higher than that of pure ZnO nanoparticles (13.48%), indicating that the introduction of NGQDs increases the C and N content (Luo et al., 2019). Fig. 2F illustrates two obvious peaks at 1020.9 and 1044.0 eV corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, in pure ZnO nanoparticles, which can be attributed to Zn2+ in the ZnO wurtzite structure. The addition of NGQDs resulted in a slight red shift in the peak positions of Zn 2p spectrum in the ZnO-NGQDs composites, which were shifted to 1021.8 and 1044.8 eV, suggesting a change in the chemical binding of Zn (Samadi, Shivaee, Zanetti, Pourjavadi, & Moshfegh, 2012). In addition, O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s spectra of the ZnO-NGQDs composites (Fig. S1) were investigated to confirm the chemical reaction between ZnO and NGQDs through ZnOC bonds during in-situ synthesis of the ZnO-NGQDs composites. The above results demonstrate the successful preparation of ZnO-NGQDs composites.

The PEC performances of ZnO nanoparticles, NGQDs, mixture of ZnO and NGQDs (ZnO/NGQDs), and ZnO-NGQDs composites were assessed in 0.1 M buffer solution (pH 7.5) at an applied potential of 0 V. As shown in Fig. 3 A, ZnO nanoparticles exhibited a photocurrent of 2.17 μA (curve a), indicating good PEC activity of ZnO under visible light. However, NGQDs exhibited a very low photocurrent of 0.006 μA (curve b), possibly due to their weak absorbance under visible light. It was interesting to note that on mixing ZnO and NGQDs, the photocurrent response of ZnO/NGQDs (curve c) was about 2.2 higher than that of ZnO. However, the ZnO-NGQDs composites (curve d) produced the largest photocurrent (19.1 μA), which was 8.8- and 3.9-fold higher than that of ZnO and ZnO/NGQDs, respectively. This can be attributed to the fact that NGQDs can improve the separation efficiency of photo-induced carriers, and further suppress the recombination of electron-holes, thereby improving the PEC performance. Our results indicate that the ZnO-NGQDs composites have excellent PEC properties.

For a more comprehensive understanding of PEC enhancement, the level structures of ZnO nanoparticles were characterized by a UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (Fig. 3B). The band gap value (Eg) of pure ZnO can be calculated according to the following equation: (αhV) n = K(hν – Eg), where α is the absorption coefficient, K is the constant for the material, hν is the photo energy, and n is 1/2 for indirect transition or 2 for direct transition (Yan et al., 2015). Fig. 3B (inset) shows the plots of (αhV)2 versus photon energy, from which the band gap value (Eg) of pure ZnO was calculated as 3.28 eV. It has previously been established that the band gap of pure NGQDs is 2.3 eV (Zhu et al., 2017). Therefore, based on previous reports (Devadoss, Sudhagar, Terashima, Nakata, & Fujishima, 2015; Moniz, Shevlin, Martin, Guo, & Tang, 2015) and the PEC spectral analysis results of this study, a possible mechanism for photocurrent enhancement is as follows (Fig. 3C): on excitation by visible light, the photogenerated electrons on the conduction band (CB) of NGQDs move to ZnO, and are then transferred to the electrode, thereby improving PEC performance. Meanwhile, the holes on the valence band (VB) of ZnO transfer to NGQDs, leaving holes on the VB of NGQDs, which can then react with O2 to generate O2
−. In this respect, the ZnO-NGQDs heterojunction can be conducive to the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as well as to the enhancement of the PEC performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of the PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the outstanding performance of the ZnO-NGQDs composites, an innovative PEC aptamer sensing strategy was developed for ZEN detection. As shown in Fig. 4 A, a strong photocurrent of 17.3 μA was achieved with ZnO-NGQDs/ITO (curve a). To eliminate the direct influence of ZEN on the PEC response, 10 ng mL−1 ZEN was dropped on the prepared electrode, denoted as ZEN/ZnO-NGQDs/ITO (curve b). No significant change was observed in the PEC current, indicating that pure ZEN does not block the PEC signal. On binding with the aptamer, the photocurrent of Apt/ZnO-NGQDs/ITO (curve c) was reduced to 12.7 μA, resulting from the weak charge transfer and steric hindrance of the aptamer. After application of 10 ng mL−1 ZEN, about 48% of the PEC signal (7.0 μA) was quenched (curve d). These results illustrate that the aptamer, coupled with the ZnO-NGQDs composites, could effectively detect ZEN.

EIS was characterized to confirm successful fabrication of the PEC aptamer sensing strategy. Fig. 4B shows the Nyquist diagrams of each electrode for the different modification steps of the PEC aptasensor. The electron-transfer resistance (Ret) of ZnO-NGQDs/ITO was 345 Ω (curve b), remarkably higher than that of the bare ITO (curve a; 160 Ω), which may be attributed to the poor conductivity of the ZnO-NGQDs composites. After modification with the aptamer and 10 ng mL−1 ZEN, the Ret of Apt/ZnO-NGQDs/ITO (curve c) and ZEN/Apt/ZnO-NGQDs/ITO (curve d) increased to 476 Ω and 597 Ω, respectively. This may be attributed to the weak electrical conductivity and steric hindrance of the aptamer and ZEN. These results are in accordance with those of the PEC response, confirming the successful construction of the PEC aptamer sensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the best analytical performance, several experimental parameters, including concentration of NGQDs, amount of ZnO-NGQDs, applied potential, aptamer concentration, pH value of the buffer solution, and incubation time between aptamer and ZEN addition, were optimized in detail (Fig. S2). Under optimal conditions, the photocurrent of the PEC aptasensor declined gradually with increasing ZEN concentration (Fig. 5 A). The ΔIPEC (ΔIPEC = I0 – I, where I0 and I represent the PEC responses in the absence and presence of ZEN, respectively) was proportional to the logarithm of ZEN concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10−7 g mL−1, with a detection limit of 3.3 × 10−14 g mL−1 (S/N = 3) (Fig. 5B). The corresponding linear regression equation was ΔIPEC = 14.86 + 1.04 log CZEN, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.991.

A comparison of the various methods available for ZEN detection is given in Table S1. It is evident that the PEC aptasensor developed in this study had a high sensitivity, almost three orders of magnitude higher than that of the traditional methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence (HPLC-FL), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). Furthermore, compared to other biosensors, including electrochemical (EC) or fluorescence (FL) biosensors based on DNA, antibodies, and aptamers, the developed method exhibited the widest linear range and relatively low detection limit. These comparisons demonstrate the superior analytical performance of the PEC aptasensor, indicating its potential for use with real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, selectivity, and reproducibility of the proposed PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To study the stability of the developed PEC aptasensor, photocurrents in the absence and presence of ZEN were recorded. As shown in Fig. 5C, no obvious changes occurred in the PEC response of the aptasensor alone and with ZEN after successive measurements for 410 s with 10 on/off irradiation cycles, which were maintained at 95.7% and 96.3% of the initial value, respectively. In addition, the long-term stability of the aptasensor was measured. Observations recorded for two weeks at 4 °C revealed that the PEC response was maintained at 93.9% of the initial photocurrent, as shown in Fig. 5D. These results suggest good stability of the PEC sensing strategy.

Furthermore, the selectivity of the prepared PEC aptasensor was assessed by comparing the PEC response with ZEN to that with other common mycotoxins, including FB1, AFB1, OTA, and their mixture. As shown in Fig. 5E, the photocurrent of 1 ng mL−1 ZEN was significantly lower (45.5% decrease) than that of the blank, while 100 ng mL−1 of the interferences caused minimal changes. The possible reason is the direct specific binding between ZEN and the aptamer. The mixture of ZEN and the interferences showed a similar PEC response (44.7%) to that of pure ZEN, indicating excellent selectivity against the interfering substances for ZEN detection. The reproducibility of the PEC aptasensor was also evaluated. Fig. 5F shows the six parallel tests used to detect the same concentration of ZEN (10 ng mL−1) with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.5%, illustrating the repeatability of the proposed method.

In order to confirm the feasibility of the PEC aptasensor in practical analyses, ZEN in cereal crops, including corn, rice, and barley flours, was estimated by the standard addition method. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of corn, rice, and barley flours ranged from 92.9% to 104.0%, 93.6% to 102.0%, and 95.8% to 107.2%, respectively. The accuracy was evaluated using triplicate experiments, with an RSD of 1.5–3.3% (corn flour), 1.2–2.1% (rice flour), and 1.2–2.5% (barley flour). In addition, ZEN was detected using the national standard method (HPLC-MS, GB 5009.209-2016) to confirm the accuracy and reliability of the PEC aptasensor. As expected, the analytical results obtained using the proposed method were consistent with those obtained using HPLC-MS, illustrating the outstanding accuracy and reliability of the PEC aptasensor in effectively quantifying the ZEN concentration in cereal crop samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating ZEN concentration in mildewing cereal crops
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the mildewing process in cereal crops (corn, rice, and barley flours), visual observation through naked eyes and ZEN quantification by the proposed PEC aptamer sensing strategy were performed under a relatively humid environment for 25 days.

It is evident from Fig. S3A that the corn flour remained unchanged till the 5th day. From the 6th day, pale yellow substances with black spots were observed, capable of producing mycotoxins during this period. By the 20th day, the mildew on the surface of the corn flour had become very prominent. No change was observed for the next few days, indicating that the mildew had reached the saturation point. Similarly, the mildew became more serious in both rice and barley flours with increasing time. Unlike corn flour, rice (Fig. S3B) and barley (Fig. S3C) flours began to produce white flocs on the 10th and 6th days, respectively.

For further understanding the mildewing process in these cereal crops, the prepared PEC aptasensor was used for specific quantification of ZEN. As shown in Fig. S4, although the ZEN concentration in cereal crops was increasing, the growth rate in each cereal crop was remarkably different. In the first three days, almost the same concentration of ZEN was produced in the three flours. However, from the 4th day, the ZEN concentration exhibited a noticeable change. For example, mildew grew the fastest in corn flour (curve a). The concentration increased with time, and by the 14th day, it had increased from 1.9 × 10−11 to 2.2 × 10−6 g mL−1. Then, the ZEN content increased slowly, which may be attributed to the saturation of ZEN produced during the mildewing process. However, the mildewing process in rice flour (curve b) was relatively slow, and the ZEN concentration increased suddenly from 1.8 × 10−11 to 1.4 × 10−6 g mL−1 after 12 days. In contrast, mildew developed at a faster rate in barley flour (curve c), but slower than that in corn flour. The concentration of ZEN in barley flour began to increase gradually from the 4th day, and tended to remain at a stable concentration of 1.2 × 10−5 g mL−1 until the 18th day.

From these results, it could be summarized that (a) the increasing trend of ZEN in mildewing cereal crops is similar to that observed by the naked eye, and corn flour is more susceptible to mildew than rice and barley flours under the same incubation environment; (b) when compared to observation with naked eyes, the proposed PEC aptamer sensing strategy can help in early detection of mildew and in quantification of ZEN in cereal crops, suggesting its potential application for early diagnosis in cereal crops.

In conclusion, an innovative PEC aptamer sensing strategy was developed for ZEN detection based on ZnO-NGQDs composites. The resultant composites exhibited a significantly higher PEC response (8.8-fold) than pure ZnO nanoparticles. The prepared PEC aptasensor exhibited improved analytical properties for ZEN quantification, including wider linear range, lower detection limit, good selectivity, and excellent accuracy. In addition, the practicability of the fabricated PEC aptasensor for ZEN analysis in spiked cereal crop samples was evaluated. The results yielded satisfactory recoveries consistent with those obtained using HPLC-MS. The developed PEC aptasensor could effectively monitor the concentration of ZEN produced during mildewing in cereal crops, indicating excellent detection and promising potential applications in cereal crops for early diagnosis.

Lijun Luo: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xiaohong Liu: Investigation, Resources. Shuai Ma: Resources, Software. Libo Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Tianyan You: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675065 and 61801195), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019T120399, 2018M632238), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province, and the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126778.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
